critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed methodology with genuine thought given to temporal leakage, label maturation, and operational realism. I'll work through the real issues I find, grouped by root cause.

---

**Group 1: Campaign Cluster Assignment and the Unseen-Campaign Evaluation**

Claim: Assigning each near-duplicate campaign cluster to the first split in which it appears prevents leakage of campaign-level signal across splits.

Why it might be wrong: The near-duplicate clustering uses normalized body, URL, and sender-template similarity — but the threshold for 'near-duplicate' is doing enormous work here and is never specified. If the threshold is too loose, genuinely distinct campaigns get collapsed into one cluster and the 'unseen campaigns' slice shrinks artificially, making the test look more novel than it is. If the threshold is too tight, variants of the same campaign that use minor obfuscation (a common evasion tactic in phishing) appear as separate clusters and bleed across splits, re-introducing the leakage the procedure was meant to prevent. There is also an asymmetry: phishing campaigns are actively designed to evade template-based detection, so sophisticated campaigns will score low similarity to their earlier variants by construction. This means the 'unseen campaign' test slice will be disproportionately populated by sophisticated, evasion-aware attacks — which is actually the right thing to test, but the methodology does not acknowledge this selection effect or its implication that the unseen-campaign slice is systematically harder, not a random sample of future traffic.

Evidence: Report the distribution of cluster sizes and the fraction of test-set positives that fall into clusters with training-set members. If nearly all test positives are in singleton clusters, the campaign-deduplication step is providing little protection and the split is closer to random than claimed. Also compare the unseen-campaign F1 against a baseline that simply flags any message whose URL or sender domain appeared in any training positive — if that trivial baseline nearly matches the model on the 'seen' slice, template memorization is doing most of the work.

---

**Group 2: The Multimodal Architecture's Prerequisite Assumption**

Claim: Fine-tuning a pretrained transformer on email subject+body and concatenating its embedding with structured features is the right integration strategy, and the resulting gain (if any) reflects semantic understanding.

Why it might be wrong: Concatenating a frozen-or-fine-tuned text embedding with structured features before an MLP assumes the two modalities are approximately equally calibrated and that the MLP can learn a useful joint representation. In practice, the transformer embedding is high-dimensional and dense while the structured features are low-dimensional and sparse; the MLP will be dominated by whichever input has larger L2 norm unless explicit normalization is applied. The methodology mentions freezing preprocessing statistics from training but does not describe normalization of the transformer embedding itself. More critically: the pretrained transformer was trained on general web text (or similar), not on email. Phishing emails are short, contain HTML artifacts, use deliberate misspellings, and embed URLs inline. Without domain-adaptive pretraining or at minimum aggressive preprocessing to expose these signals, the transformer may not produce embeddings that are more informative than the lexical features the boosted tree already sees (urgency phrases, brand impersonation patterns, obfuscation markers). The methodology acknowledges this at the end ('it does not by itself prove that any gain comes specifically from deeper semantic understanding') but does not design a diagnostic to distinguish the two cases — it just removes URL/domain reputation features, which is a different confound.

A specific failure mode: if the transformer learns to attend to URL tokens in the body (which are highly predictive), its 'semantic' gain is actually URL memorization routed through attention rather than lexical features — and this is not separated out by the URL reputation ablation, because that ablation removes the structured reputation feature, not the raw URL tokens from the text input.

Evidence: Run the transformer on text with URLs masked/replaced with a [URL] token. Compare against full text. If F1 drops substantially, the transformer's advantage is URL token memorization, not semantic understanding. Also report the L2 norm ratio of text embedding vs. structured feature vectors at the MLP input layer to check for modality dominance.

---

**Group 3: F1 as Primary Metric Under Severe Imbalance with a Fixed Threshold**

Claim: Event-level F1 with threshold selected on the validation set gives a direct, operationally meaningful comparison between the two models.

Why this has a subtle flaw: F1 is sensitive to the prevalence at threshold-selection time. The validation set (months 9-10) may have a different phishing rate than the test set (months 11-12) if there were campaign surges, takedowns, or shifts in attacker behavior over the year. A threshold optimized for F1 on one prevalence will be miscalibrated at another prevalence — precision and recall move in opposite directions, so F1 can fall even if the model's underlying discrimination improves. The methodology correctly freezes thresholds from validation and reports recall at fixed FPR (which is threshold-independent), but then uses F1 as the primary model selection criterion, creating an inconsistency: you are selecting models by a threshold-dependent metric and then evaluating them with that same frozen threshold on a potentially shifted prevalence. If the phishing rate in months 11-12 is meaningfully different from months 9-10, F1 rankings between the two models on the test set may not reflect their true relative discrimination ability.

Evidence: Report the phishing prevalence (positive rate) separately for the validation and test periods. If they differ by more than ~30% relative, recompute F1 at the test-set prevalence using a recalibrated threshold and check whether model rankings change. PR-AUC, which is already reported, is threshold-independent and should be used as the tiebreaker for model selection if F1 rankings are ambiguous.

---

**Group 4: Label Noise Asymmetry Between Positives and the Censored Negative Population**

Claim: The 21-day maturation window adequately addresses label censoring, and the resulting negative set is a reliable representation of benign mail.

Why it might be wrong: The maturation window handles false negatives from slow-maturing labels, but it does not handle a different form of censoring: emails that triggered no analyst review, no sandbox, no user report, and caused no incident are treated as confirmed negatives. In an enterprise environment, this is a large population — but it includes emails that were phishing but evaded all detection and reporting. These are exactly the hard positives. Labeling them as negatives is not a conservative error; it is training the model to classify the hardest phishing as benign. The 14-day incident linkage window for positives partially mitigates this, but only for phishing that eventually resulted in a confirmed incident. Phishing that was clicked, led to credential theft, but was never linked back to a specific email within 14 days remains in the negative set. The methodology does not quantify this contamination rate or assess its impact.

Evidence: Estimate the contamination rate by checking what fraction of emails in the 'negative' training set came from sender domains or URL patterns that appear in confirmed positive campaigns (allowing a time lag). If this fraction is non-trivial (say >1% of the negative set, given that positives are rare), the model is being trained on a noisier negative set than assumed, and reported precision figures will be systematically optimistic because the 'false positives' at evaluation time may include true phishing labeled as negative.
all_issues_raised: The near-duplicate clustering threshold is unspecified and directly controls whether the 'unseen campaign' test slice reflects genuine generalization or residual template leakage — evasion-aware campaigns will systematically populate the unseen slice, but this selection effect is unacknowledged.

Concatenating the transformer embedding with structured features without specifying embedding normalization risks MLP modality dominance, and URL tokens in raw email body text could produce a 'semantic gain' that is actually URL memorization not separated by the structured-feature ablation.

F1 is used as the primary model selection criterion with a threshold frozen from validation, but if phishing prevalence shifts between the validation and test periods, threshold miscalibration will distort F1 rankings in a direction unrelated to model discrimination quality.

Emails that triggered no detection or reporting are labeled as confirmed negatives, but this population includes hard phishing that evaded all monitoring — contaminating the negative training set with the most evasion-capable attacks and biasing reported precision upward.

F1 is used as the primary model selection criterion with a threshold frozen from validation, but if phishing prevalence shifts between the validation and test periods, threshold miscalibration will distort F1 rankings in a direction unrelated to model discrimination quality.

Emails that triggered no detection or reporting are labeled as confirmed negatives, but this population includes hard phishing that evaded all monitoring — contaminating the negative training set with the most evasion-capable attacks and biasing reported precision upward.
